source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure That Separation of Duties Is Enforced While Assigning Service Account Related Roles to Users

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure That Separation of Duties Is Enforced While Assigning Service Account Related Roles to Users
input_row.description: It is recommended that the principle of 'Separation of Duties' is enforced while assigning service-account related roles to users.
input_row.rationale: The built-in/predefined IAM role Service Account admin allows the user/identity to create, delete, and manage service account(s). The built-in/predefined IAM role Service Account User allows the user/identity (with adequate privileges on Compute and App Engine) to assign service account(s) to Apps/Compute Instances. Separation of duties is the concept of ensuring that one individual does not have all necessary permissions to be able to complete a malicious action. In Cloud IAM - service accounts, this could be an action such as using a service account to access resources that user should not normally have access to. Separation of duties is a business control typically used in larger organizations, meant to help avoid security or privacy incidents and errors. It is considered best practice. No user should have Service Account Admin and Service Account User roles assigned at the same time. Impact: The removed role should be assigned to a different user based on business needs.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to IAM & Admin/IAM using https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/iam. 2. Ensure no member has the roles Service Account Admin and Service account User assigned together. From Google Cloud CLI 1. List all users and role assignments: gcloud projects get-iam-policy [Project_ID] --format json | \ jq -r '[ (["Service_Account_Admin_and_User"] | (., map(length*"-"))), ( [ .bindings[] | select(.role == "roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin" or .role == "roles/iam.serviceAccountUser").members[] ] | group_by(.) | map({User: ., Count: length}) | .[] | select(.Count == 2).User | unique ) ] | .[] | @tsv' 2. All common users listed under Service_Account_Admin_and_User are assigned both the roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin and roles/iam.serviceAccountUser roles.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to IAM & Admin/IAM using https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/iam. 2. For any member having both Service Account Admin and Service account User roles granted/assigned, click the Delete Bin icon to remove either role from the member. Removal of a role should be done based on the business requirements. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts 2. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles 3. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-roles-to-service-accounts Additional Information: Users granted with Owner (roles/owner) and Editor (roles/editor) have privileges equivalent to Service Account Admin and Service Account User. To avoid the misuse, Owner and Editor roles should be granted to very limited users and Use of these primitive privileges should be minimal. These requirements are addressed in separate recommendations.
input_row.impact: The removed role should be assigned to a different user based on business needs.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts 2. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles 3. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-roles-to-service-accounts Additional Information: Users granted with Owner (roles/owner) and Editor (roles/editor) have privileges equivalent to Service Account Admin and Service Account User. To avoid the misuse, Owner and Editor roles should be granted to very limited users and Use of these primitive privileges should be minimal. These requirements are addressed in separate recommendations.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control can be fully verified using GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands, making it suitable for an automated audit approach. The control revolves around checking the IAM roles assigned to users in a GCP project, specifically ensuring that no user has both the `Service Account Admin` and `Service Account User` roles simultaneously. This task is well-suited for automation as the IAM settings can be queried directly using GCP CLI tools.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_iam_roles_service_account_separation_of_duties_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP API Calls/Google Cloud SDK Methods**: Use `gcloud iam roles` and `gcloud projects get-iam-policy`
- **GCP CLI Commands**: 
  - Retrieve the list of all IAM roles assigned in a project using:
    ```bash
    gcloud projects get-iam-policy [Project_ID] --format=json
    ```
  - Use `jq` or an equivalent tool to process the JSON output and identify users holding both `roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser`.
- **Specific Resources to Enumerate**: All IAM role bindings in the specified GCP project.
- **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate**: Check users with both roles `roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` assigned concurrently.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria**:
  - **Pass**: No users have both `Service Account Admin` and `Service Account User` roles assigned.
  - **Fail**: One or more users have both roles assigned.

This automated approach ensures that separation of duties is enforced for service account-related roles, enhancing security and minimizing the risk of unauthorized access or misuse.
step2_review:
---

### 1. **APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. This control can be fully evaluated using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). IAM role bindings are accessible via `gcloud projects get-iam-policy`, and the logic to check for users with both `roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` can be scripted.
- **GCP SDK Capabilities:**  
  The Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) and REST APIs fully support querying and enumerating IAM policy bindings. No need for manual review unless there are complex, undocumented exceptions.
- **Other Tools:**  
  GCP does not have a native equivalent to AWS Config Rules for this specific check, but the approach using gcloud and jq is valid.

---

### 2. **TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The use of `gcloud projects get-iam-policy [PROJECT_ID] --format=json` is correct and standard for extracting IAM bindings.
  - Processing with `jq` or similar is appropriate for automation.
  - The check for both `roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` is accurate.
- **Program Name:**  
  - The program name, `gcp_iam_roles_service_account_separation_of_duties_enforced`, is descriptive but could be more precise. It does not specify whether it is checking for *concurrent assignment* or *enforcement*.
  - Naming could be improved for clarity and alignment with best practice (see below).
- **Completeness:**  
  - The assessment does not mention checking for custom roles that aggregate both permissions, which is a possible gap.
  - The approach does not specify handling for service accounts themselves being assigned these roles (should only apply to users?).
  - No mention of checking at the organization or folder level, which could be relevant if IAM inheritance is in use.

---

### 3. **IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Custom Roles:**  
  - The check should also consider custom roles that might include both `iam.serviceAccounts.*` (admin) and `iam.serviceAccountUser` permissions.  
    *Improvement*: Query for custom roles and their permissions using `gcloud iam roles describe [ROLE_ID]` or `gcloud iam roles list --project=[PROJECT_ID]`.
- **Scope:**  
  - Consider checking IAM bindings at the organization and folder levels, not just the project.
- **Service Accounts as Principals:**  
  - Clarify whether service accounts themselves (as principals) should be included/excluded from this check.
- **Program Name:**  
  - Suggest:  
    `gcp_iam_user_service_account_dual_role_assignment`  
    or  
    `gcp_iam_user_service_account_admin_user_separation_check`
  - This makes it clear the check is for *users* (not service accounts), and that it is about *dual assignment*.
- **Alternative API Methods:**  
  - For large orgs, consider using [Cloud Asset Inventory](https://cloud.google.com/asset-inventory/docs/quickstart) to query IAM policies across all resources:
    ```bash
    gcloud asset search-all-iam-policies --scope=organizations/[ORG_ID] --query='policy:(roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin AND roles/iam.serviceAccountUser)'
    ```
- **Documentation:**  
  - Recommend documenting the logic for handling group memberships (i.e., if a user is a member of a group assigned one role and directly assigned another).

---

### 4. **CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is technically correct for standard GCP environments and leverages supported APIs/SDKs.
- Minor improvements needed for completeness and edge cases.

---

### 5. **FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** **Modify**

- **Keep the automated approach.**
- **Modify** the automation logic to:
  - Check for custom roles that aggregate both sets of permissions.
  - Clarify and document the treatment of service accounts as principals.
  - Optionally, expand the scope to organization/folder-level IAM bindings if applicable.
  - Use a more precise program name as suggested above.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section              | Assessment         | Notes                                                                                 |
|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation  | AGREE             | Automated is correct; GCP APIs/SDKs fully support this check.                         |
| Technical Accuracy   | 8/10              | Accurate use of gcloud; needs custom roles and scope considerations.                  |
| Improvements         | See above         | Custom roles, org/folder scope, service accounts as principals, program name.         |
| Confidence           | HIGH              | Standard GCP APIs and methods; minor edge cases only.                                 |
| Final Recommendation | MODIFY            | Keep automated, but improve logic and naming for completeness and clarity.            |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identified that this control can be fully audited using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The IAM policy bindings are directly accessible and can be programmatically checked to ensure no user has both the Service Account Admin and Service Account User roles concurrently. No manual inspection is required, as all necessary data is exposed via the API.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_iam_policy_service_account_dual_role_assignment_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: IAM Policy Bindings (Project-level)
- API/CLI Method:  
  - `gcloud projects get-iam-policy [PROJECT_ID] --format=json`
- Properties to Check:  
  - For each member, check if both `roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` are assigned.
- Pass Criteria:  
  - No member (user, group, or service account) has both `roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` assigned at the same time.
- Fail Criteria:  
  - One or more members have both roles assigned concurrently.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There were no substantive disagreements between the two AI opinions. The only minor difference was in the suggested program name; I selected a name that is both precise and descriptive, clearly indicating the dual-role assignment check.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
